content:
Hi!

Wrt: https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-bindgen/issues/1949#issuecomment-1887678258 I am thinking of trying my hands at a port of bindgen to use GCC backend. I'm still waiting on the bindgen maintainers to accept the proposal.

I'm starting this thread for further discussion / gathering resources for better understanding.
I'm not an expert with GCC internals / bindgen internals but I definitely want to learn and try at the very least with this attempt. I noticed that a few rustc_codegen_gcc contributors are here so I would really appreciate any and all help :heart:

I don't think any of us are bindgen experts :smile: The bindgen maintainers would likely have no reason to reject a GCC backend, but they are a bit thin on maintenance so response times are between days and months.

So, I really wouldn't wait on their response to start your work. If the maintainers say no (again, unlikely), it would probably lead to a community-supported GCC version - definitely no effort wasted
The first step would be to figure out how to cleanly abstract the LLVM interfaces to a single trait entrypoint so it is easy to plug another backend in place

I think they do have a reason to reject a GCC backend -- extra maintenance.

In the chance they do say no I was wondering if we could maintain a C only bindgen translator using GCC backend. The kernel doesn't use C++ anyways.

Trevor Gross said:

I don't think any of us are bindgen experts :smile: The bindgen maintainers would likely have no reason to reject a GCC backend, but they are a bit thin on maintenance so response times are between days and months.

So, I really wouldn't wait on their response to start your work. If the maintainers say no (again, unlikely), it would probably lead to a community-supported GCC version - definitely no effort wasted

Trevor Gross said:

The first step would be to figure out how to cleanly abstract the LLVM interfaces to a single trait entrypoint so it is easy to plug another backend in place

I'll look into it. I think I'll start out with understanding the overall flow of bindgen internals since I'm very new to it apart from using it in projects.

You could submit something like that as its own PR first and collect feedback, before getting too much into the work of adding GCC. Other compiler backends should be possible too down the road.

There is a rustc-codegen-gcc stream on the rust-lang Zulip https://rust-lang.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/rustc-codegen-gcc. I would recommend asking for GCC-specific help there rather than here (there is some overlap of members e.g @antoyo, but in general the experts on GCC internals hang out there)
Awesome! Thanks for the tip.
The GCC Rust team is another option for the GCC internals questions etc. (https://gcc-rust.zulipchat.com, Cc @Arthur Cohen)
If anyone is interested in joining this effort then please do let me know :smile: . I'm gonna make a GitHub repo once I'm done with some analysis of bindgen internals.
( network drop, sorry )

I agree with Trevor that an initial prototype of a GCC backend would be a good starting point. Even if it does not support everything, it would still be useful to quickstart things and to help the bindgen team evaluate the cost.

Miguel Ojeda said:

I agree with Trevor that an initial prototype of a GCC backend would be a good starting point. Even if it does not support everything, it would still be useful to quickstart things and to help the bindgen team evaluate the cost.

I'll try my best, I'll get back to this thread after some poking around if that's okay.
Of course, thanks for looking into it! Getting the ball rolling on that would be great :)
I'm just a university student so I don't expect to be of much help but I'll try my best :smile: thanks for the resources again :heart:

Worth to mention is that C/C++ parsing is not supported in libgccjit, but I think that there could be a way to convert the C/C++ backends at gcc/c and gcc/cx into libraries, much like libgccjit did for codegen.
In the future there could be libgccc and libgccc++ like what libclang does.
Maybe one day your work can be used to plug something for MSVC too, running bindgen on Windows is a massive pain

Martin Rodriguez Reboredo said:

Worth to mention is that C/C++ parsing is not supported in libgccjit, but I think that there could be a way to convert the C/C++ backends at gcc/c and gcc/cx into libraries, much like libgccjit did for codegen.

I was planning to read up on gcc internals from their wiki. I will look into this and ask questions if I'm lost. Thanks for the tip

Martin Rodriguez Reboredo said:

but I think that there could be a way to convert the C/C++ backends at gcc/c and gcc/cx into libraries, much like libgccjit did for codegen.

Is GCC planning more to make more things available as a library? Or do you mean as a fork / maintained separately?
I was saying it as a proposal of mine, I don't know if the GCC devs thought the same.
I'll toy around with the concept of extracting this out of the cli code. Not sure if gnu will accept the proposal but I guess I can only try and see.

Martin Rodriguez Reboredo said:

I was saying it as a proposal of mine, I don't know if the GCC devs thought the same.

Ah, I see, thanks. I was asking because my understanding is that GCC explicitly avoids making certain parts modular (or did in the past at least).
The C++ backend Makefile would be worth checking out, particularly, the CXX_OBJS variable contains all the files for the C++ language.
Ideally we only need to care for C backend right? The kernel isn't using C++ at all as far as I'm aware.
Bindgen uses C++, that's why I've suggested the C++ backend.

What if we use neither of them, not libclang and not gcc. It should be possible to implement a simple C language parser in Rust in a reasonable amount time since there are no C++ in the kernel. The only issue that I see is that we have to know the precise ABI information to generate correct struct sizes. Is there any way to get this info without linking to gcc or clang?

I was considering this approach but then we need to keep track of which c standard we support along with all gcc extensions which can get tedious very quickly. Please correct me if my understanding is flawed.
Sorry, I also don't know, but I think it only matters if we have some strange macro as part of a struct declaration. For this task we don't care what happens inside the function bodies.

Another thing I was considering, currently bindgen doesn't support any macro conversion apart from basic defines from what I could tell in my limited testing. Would support for it be meaningful/useful?

bindgen currently lacks a proper C preprocessor, so it's not able to expand a basic constant with "BIT(x)" macro. bindgen has an expression evaluator using cexpr crate which is ok-ish, I think if we implement a preprocessor expansion pass on the macros before sending them to cexpr it should be able to evaluate most of the constants.

Vadzim Dambrouski said:

What if we use neither of them, not libclang and not gcc. It should be possible to implement a simple C language parser in Rust in a reasonable amount time since there are no C++ in the kernel. The only issue that I see is that we have to know the precise ABI information to generate correct struct sizes. Is there any way to get this info without linking to gcc or clang?

There are many C parsers out there (e.g. sparse that Linus started), but the issue is really interpreting the code exactly as GCC does: it is not just GNU extensions or conditional compilation, but everything that may change the ABI as you say. For instance, one may have enabled layout randomization or one may be using a plugin or a fork etc.

So one way would be to "just" ask GCC to give us the information. Otherwise, if we are able to use another parser, one could simply use Clang (perhaps with extra information that we lack). If we do the latter (i.e. what we are doing now), one very good thing to have (suggested by Nick) is checking after the fact (via e.g. DWARF) that things match.

Aakash Sen Sharma said:

Another thing I was considering, currently bindgen doesn't support any macro conversion apart from basic defines from what I could tell in my limited testing. Would support for it be meaningful/useful?

There is definitely some things that would be useful in bindgen -- please check the issue list we have at https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/353, it contains some pointers.
Thats a good point, thank you. Does Rust for linux currently work with randomize_layout enabled ?

We currently do not allow enabling RANDSTRUCT. It may work with the new Clang's attribute in Clang 16/17 (15 is disabled in the kernel), but it is untested. It would be nice to allow to enable it if it works.
Hey, apologies if this is a stupid question but what's the use of randomize_layout? I don't think I understand it completely.

It randomizes the order struct fields at compile time. Makes the exploitation of memory corruption vulnerabilities a lot harder as you can no longer predict the type layout, e.g., to create fake instances or to manipulate fields of live instances. Note: Afaik the randomization is still applied after emitting debug info, i.e., it is useless.
Meant that the generated debug info is "useless", in the sense that it does not reflect the randomization.

This command echo | gcc -dM -E - reports all predefined macros set by the compiler for this specific architecture and flags, among them sizes in bits for all data types, like __LONG_LONG_WIDTH__ 64 and __SIZEOF_LONG_LONG__ 8. I still think this may be enough information if combined with a C parser to get some results. Minus gcc plugins of course, but they can be handled separately. I need to find some counter example where this is not enough.
Attributes won't put up a fight?
Can you please share which attribute may be hard to handle?

My current plan is as follows: take the original bindings_helper.h file and generate two new files:

    $(CC) $(c_flags) -x c -E -o $@.preproc $<
    $(CC) $(c_flags) -x c -dM -E -o $@.macros $<

One is preprocessed C source which already has all the macros expanded and #ifdefs resolved, it should be relatively easy to parse. The other file contains all the macro definitions including functional and non functional macros. From this file we can learn the sizes for all basic types and also generate the constants.
That $@ should be a $*, but I get the point. I've mentioned attributes because I had problems with them in the past, and I don't know if they'd be easy to deal with when not using GCC or Clang.

Started parsing the file, found some interesting declarations, for example

register unsigned long current_stack_pointer asm ("sp");

didn't know you can do that

Now this is a problem:

  struct {
   u8 __padding[(__builtin_offsetof(struct raw_spinlock, dep_map))];
   struct lockdep_map dep_map;
  };

I see that bindgen also skips the spinlock, maybe I can just skip it too, because implementing __builtin_offsetof is too much work
hm, but if I skip it then I wouldn't know the total size of spinlock struct. It's a bigger problem than I thought.

Minus gcc plugins of course, but they can be handled separately.

But those bits are the problematic part (like the randomized layout plugin, but in general GCC-only features; and no just today's features, but features introduced in the future too at any time). In other words, if you can use a non-GCC parser (i.e. assuming it is enough for a solution), then you could just use Clang's parser, and at that point you could use bindgen as-is.

A different approach like the one you suggest could still be nice to remove the LLVM/Clang dependency for those that would prefer to avoid it, of course, and potentially to do things that bindgen does not support.

By the way, if you think knowing sizes etc. would be enough, and you already know somehow (e.g. with your parser) the entities you need to ask the size/alignment/offsets/... for, then you could try generating and compiling a C file where you query the actual target compiler via sizeof etc.

That's a great idea! Initially I was planning to do this as a last step, after everything is parsed and all sizes are determined we emit 2 files one .rs with bindings and a C source full of _Static_assert declarations which are then compiled with a target compiler to check that we did everything correctly. But now as I see some structs depend on sizeof and __builtin_offsetof we may need to generate an additional file for query. I'm not sure how to do that yet because generally we are not allowed to run the code as we may be cross compiling for a different architecture, so we need to get the info at compile time or from dwarf if it's possible.

Also I think implementing support for arbitrary gcc plugin may be a bit easier with the custom parser because you are not limited by the clang API that may get in the way for some versions of clang.

Ok, I have an idea how to do this! First I generate a C file like this:

struct Foo {
    char bar;
    int baz;
};

unsigned long long __MY_LAYOUT_INFO[] = {
    0xabcdef, // some hash of name "Foo" for robustness
    sizeof(struct Foo),
    __builtin_offsetof(struct Foo, bar),
    __builtin_offsetof(struct Foo, baz),
};

Then I compile it as normal with -S flag to generate assembly output. Then I just find label __MY_LAYOUT_INFO: and start parsing numbers to get all the info I need. It should work for both gcc and clang and respect all the compile flags.

Vadzim Dambrouski said:

I'm not sure how to do that yet because generally we are not allowed to run the code as we may be cross compiling for a different architecture, so we need to get the info at compile time or from dwarf if it's possible.

Yeah, you only need to compile it, not run it (unless you wanted to do it via printf or similar, but it is not needed, you can inspect the generated object file one way or another).

Vadzim Dambrouski said:

Also I think implementing support for arbitrary gcc plugin may be a bit easier with the custom parser because you are not limited by the clang API that may get in the way for some versions of clang.

In that case, we could also extend Clang's API. I asked the Clang maintainer and he was kind enough to offer to review changes meant for this purpose.

In addition, if a user is already using a GCC plugin, one would not lose much by doing it via another GCC plugin.

Vadzim Dambrouski said:

Ok, I have an idea how to do this! First I generate a C file like this:

struct Foo {
    char bar;
    int baz;
};

unsigned long long __MY_LAYOUT_INFO[] = {
    0xabcdef, // some hash of name "Foo" for robustness
    sizeof(struct Foo),
    __builtin_offsetof(struct Foo, bar),
    __builtin_offsetof(struct Foo, baz),
};

Yeah, that is what I meant by querying! :)
Do you think such info would work fine with layout randomization? I hope we don't even need to know about the plugin.
Are there any usage of offsetof on a layout randomized struct in one of headers supplied to bindgen? If not then you can be assure that we won't have to deal with it.

Ok, I have an idea how to do this! First I generate a C file like this:

struct Foo {
    char bar;
    int baz;
};

unsigned long long __MY_LAYOUT_INFO[] = {
    0xabcdef, // some hash of name "Foo" for robustness
    sizeof(struct Foo),
    __builtin_offsetof(struct Foo, bar),
    __builtin_offsetof(struct Foo, baz),
};

Are bitfields a problem?
I didn't look into bitfields yet, do you know if there is a way to query them?

Yes, it fails on bitfileds, need some way around it

<source>:13:31: error: attempt to take address of bit-field structure member 'x'
   13 |     __builtin_offsetof(struct Foo, x),
      |                               ^~~

Ok for bitfileds I can output something like this and it will give the exact location of each bit field in memory

struct Bar {
    char a;
    int bit : 2;
    int sts : 1;
    char b;
};
struct Bar __MY_LAYOUT_INFO_Bar_bit = {.bit = 0x3 };
struct Bar __MY_LAYOUT_INFO_Bar_sts = {.sts = 0x1 };

Hi, I have been experimenting with minor attempts at extracting the c parser from gcc. Given all the experimentation and other approaches being explored here, should I continue with it or invest my effort in vadzims approach?

Sorry, it's just me being noisy. I am still trying to confirm that my approach would work at all. I am collecting the list of issues that needs to be solved, but I can't parse the source file fully yet so there are some things I don't know yet. Please wait a bit, I'll try to make some prototype first to confirm things.
Sure I'll wait for the results of your testing. It's definitely not noisy! Thank you for your work

Aakash Sen Sharma said:

Another thing I was considering, currently bindgen doesn't support any macro conversion apart from basic defines from what I could tell in my limited testing. Would support for it be meaningful/useful?

There is a PR to add this, but it is huge and blocked on something else that is also pretty big. https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-bindgen/pull/2369

I just found out that the approach I am trying to figure out is similar to what cgo does in golang. They even use the same compile flags :) I'll just put this link here if anyone interested. https://github.com/golang/go/blob/860c9c0b8df6c0a2849fdd274a0a9f142cba3ea5/src/cmd/cgo/doc.go#L378-L471
Except I'm not going to parse error messages, only the assembly output. I think parsing error messages is a bad idea, and it's better to just write a simple C parser.

Yeah, they took parsing error messages because they wanted to avoid using a full blown C parser, but that will not happen here because that would be painful to deal with and bindgen already requires C parsing.

Does anyone know if there are unicode characters in identifiers anywhere in the kernel? I just learned that C11 allows unicode. Now I have to know should I try to parse it or stick to ascii?

Annex D, ISO/IEC 9899:201x https://www.sigbus.info/n1570#D

Wow, that's interesting.

Don't go out of your way to add unicode support if it isn't already in bindgen, that's an upstream problem (worth opening an issue if there isn't one)
I have to imagine that kernel reviewers are pretty anti-unicode-idents anyway
Rust actually has #![forbid(non_ascii_idents)], not a bad thing to have enabled for kernel code

As I understand currently bindgen works with strings coming from libclang, which are all utf8. However my parser has to work with raw source code, which can be anything. I currently treat it as &[u8] and only work with ascii symbols. In theory this should work fine even if there are some utf8 characters in the source. I don't really need to check if identifiers have valid characters in them as I already know that the source code is valid, only need to parse it.

When I'm writing parsers in Rust I almost always use &[u8] everywhere, and just str::from_utf8(...) when I actually have a reason to (e.g. right before storage in a struct when I want to be able to display it)
https://docs.rs/bstr/latest/bstr/ can make these things a bit nicer
But I wouldn't go too out of your way to support utf8 strings if it adds any work. You can pretty easily assume you'll never run into them

Trevor Gross said:

Rust actually has #![forbid(non_ascii_idents)], not a bad thing to have enabled for kernel code

We compile the kernel with -Dnon_ascii_idents, so no need to worry about those.

Martin Rodriguez Reboredo said:

I was saying it as a proposal of mine, I don't know if the GCC devs thought the same.

sorry for replying so late, but please note that this has been attempted multiple times in the past and the contributor failed to finish it every time. the amount of globals that are used in GCC is insane, and it is very hard to abstract it all as a library. however, this is something that the rustc_codegen_gcc maintainers and I are very interested in, so if you need any help/getting started we'd love to help
I pitched the proposal to use the C/C++ frontend as a library only as an idea, I'm not going to work on it for now, but I appreciate your willingness nonetheless.

Hello. I was a bit busy with my day job for the last two weeks, so there was no substantial progress on the C parser, but I am going to spend this weekend working on it for sure. My assumption that you can just sit and write a simple C parser turned out to be incorrect unfortunately :(. If anyone interested here is a great blog post on many issues one may face when parsing C.

https://blog.robertelder.org/building-broken-c-parsers/

I had to scrap my first prototype and start a new one with a slightly different approach, I hope it would work out.

The new approach seems to be working well for now. The idea is to parse the file in 2 stages, first stage only collects tokens into groups separated by semicolons and nested by (){}[]. Then on the second stage we can parse each group of tokens separately to extract the declarations. The first stage can't fail unless you have unbalanced parenthesis (which is not valid in C anyway). This allows us to precisely skip over all the complicated syntax inside the function bodies and other declarations we don't care about and only parse what we need. The second advantage is that on the second stage if we failed to parse some declaration we can just ignore it and skip over to the next one. It's not a big deal to skip some struct which failed to parse, we don't need to abort the whole process.

This weekend I implemented the first stage parser. It's able to parse all of the bindings_helper.h file into a tree and then serialize it back to the stage1.c file without introducing any syntax errors. I put the code on github if anyone interested

https://github.com/pftbest/kbindgen

I also started parsing some things for the second stage but there is nothing concrete for now, hopefully I will have more time next weekend.

This week I implemented proper parsing for typedefs and structs, including all weird ones with arrays, commas and function pointers. All struct members are parsed recursively. Now only 2 things are missing, enum variants and function arguments. I will add them next week and we can start generating queries for size and alignments!

I added support for enums and function arguments, now the parser is done! At least I know all the names, types and functions, however now I need to figure out how to query all the info that is useful for the bindings. Also I'll need to write another parser to collect the results from the asm output (should be easy). And a third parser for the #define macros :)
Just curious, will you need a parser for each cpu arch?
Needs to be tested of course, but I think data declarations look the same for most architectures. I'll try to make it as universal as possible
For example I tried it on godbolt and there are some differences in keywords but it's very similar https://godbolt.org/z/4sdhW69j3
If you know some architecture that is completely different please share

I just found the most complicated macro in the kernel. It's called GENMASK. For example a simple constant like this

#define PMSCR_EL1_PCT GENMASK(7, 6)

Expands to this expression:

((((int)(sizeof(struct { int:(-!!(__builtin_choose_expr( (sizeof(int) == sizeof(*(8 ? ((void *)((long)((6) > (7)) * 0l)) : (int *)8))), (6) > (7), 0))); })))) + (((~(((0UL)))) - ((((1UL))) << (6)) + 1) & (~(((0UL))) >> (64 - 1 - (7)))))

Which contains all sorts of stuff and at the end evaluates to a simple integer 192 :)

Here is the list of all macros from the bindings_helper.h which are considered constant expressions by the gcc.
https://gist.githubusercontent.com/pftbest/e99e0fdeecfeee2e4533c927e65e20b0/raw/1a0d93c3ab760b800a3e4e11490f27b8ca99880e/all.h
Ideally we need to parse and evaluate them all.

Here is the list of macros which are causing compile errors or are not considered constant by the __builtin_constant_p call:
https://gist.github.com/pftbest/d948e310502f9553cecf56c141631ab6
Some of them are struct initializers, but I'm inclined to ignore them for now.

As I can only query values that are constant and don't cause compile errors, I need to be able to tell for each macro if it is constant or not and it's type. Not sure how to do that yet.

What about adding a flag to GCC and Clang that dumps all the necessary information to a JSON file?

This has a few advantages:

  • Avoids depending on internal GCC or Clang API/ABI.
  • Can be implemented by any compiler, not just GCC or Clang.
  • Can be used by any tool, not just bindgen.

Yes, such solution would be great if implemented for gcc and clang, but I'm not familiar with gcc internals to do it like this. I am currently trying to implement something that is much simpler and works with current versions of clang and gcc without relying on internal apis. But there are still some unresolved problems like how evaluate macros.
If you can implement such option for the gcc please do, I think it would be helpful not only for Rust but other languages as well.
It is awesome that you are making progress on this :smile:

Vadzim Dambrouski said:

The new approach seems to be working well for now. The idea is to parse the file in 2 stages, first stage only collects tokens into groups separated by semicolons and nested by (){}[]. Then on the second stage we can parse each group of tokens separately to extract the declarations. The first stage can't fail unless you have unbalanced parenthesis (which is not valid in C anyway).

Btw, looks like you might have already figured this out but the first stage is known as "lexing" (splitting identifiers, punctuation, string literals), "parsing" is the second stage where you turn it into a usable AST/CST

The rustc parser is an incredible reference if you need inspiration. The entrypoint for lexing a single token is here https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/b6d2d841bcf4b77343b159f134c7d39c2dd4ceaa/compiler/rustc_lexer/src/lib.rs#L333, which gets called within this StringReader (kind of lexer+) https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/b6d2d841bcf4b77343b159f134c7d39c2dd4ceaa/compiler/rustc_parse/src/lexer/mod.rs#L121, then it goes to the parser https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/blob/b6d2d841bcf4b77343b159f134c7d39c2dd4ceaa/compiler/rustc_parse/src/parser/mod.rs#L248-L296

Thanks! FYI, I already implemented parsing for types and declarations, now I am trying to figure out how to query the info about sizes and alignments, and how to evaluate macros and other constant expressions like array sizes :)
I do have a kind of a lexer called tokenizer https://github.com/pftbest/kbindgen/blob/main/src/tokenizer.rs#L278
the difference between it and what I called the 1st stage is that It returns just a linear list of tokens, but the 1st stage builds a nested tree of groups, similar to what we get as an input to the proc_macro in Rust. It's not the most efficient solution to do this in multiple stages, but it lets me recover much easier from unsupported syntax like for example inline assembly at the end of declarations
